From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Distress Tolerance Handout 7 (Distress Tolerance Worksheets 5â€“5b)
Distracting
A way to remember these skills is the phrase â€śWise Mind ACCEPTS.â€ť
With Activities:

Â‰ Focus attention on a task you need to get
done.

Â‰ Rent movies; watch TV.

Â‰ Clean a room in your house.

Â‰ Find an event to go to.

Â‰ Play computer games.

Â‰ Go walking. Exercise.

Â‰ Surf the Internet. Write e-mails.

Â‰ Play sports.

Â‰ Go out for a meal or eat a favorite food.

Â‰ Call or go out with a friend.

Â‰ Listen to your iPod; download music.

Â‰ Build something.

Â‰ Spend time with your children.

Â‰ Play cards.

Â‰ Read magazines, books, comics.

Â‰ Do crossword puzzles or Sudoku.

Â‰ Other:
With Contributing:

Â‰ Find volunteer work to do.

Â‰ Help a friend or family member.

Â‰ Surprise someone with something nice (a
card, a favor, a hug).

Â‰ Give away things you donâ€™t need.

Â‰ Call or send an instant message encouraging
someone or just saying hi.

Â‰ Make something nice for someone else.

Â‰ Do something thoughtful.

Â‰ Other:
With Comparisons:

Â‰ Compare how you are feeling now to a time
when you felt different.

Â‰ Think about people coping the same as you or
less well than you.

Â‰ Compare yourself to those less fortunate.

Â‰ Watch reality shows about othersâ€™ troubles;
read about disasters, othersâ€™ suffering.

Â‰ Other:
With different Emotions:

Â‰ Read emotional books or stories, old letters.

Â‰ Watch emotional TV shows; go to emotional
movies.

Â‰ Listen to emotional music.
(Be sure the event creates different emotions.)
Ideas: Scary movies, joke books, comedies,
funny records, religious music, soothing music
or music that fires you up, going to a store and
reading funny greeting cards.

Â‰ Other:
With Pushing away:

Â‰ Push the situation away by leaving it for a
while.

Â‰ Leave the situation mentally.

Â‰ Build an imaginary wall between yourself and
the situation.

Â‰ Block thoughts and images from your mind.

Â‰ Notice ruminating: Yell â€śNo!â€ť

Â‰ Refuse to think about the painful situations.

Â‰ Put the pain on a shelf. Box it up and put it
away for a while.

Â‰ Deny the problem for the moment.

Â‰ Other:
With other Thoughts:

Â‰ Count to 10; count colors in a painting or
poster or out the window; count anything.

Â‰ Repeat words to a song in your mind.

Â‰ Work puzzles.

Â‰ Watch TV or read.

Â‰ Other:
With other Sensations:

Â‰ Squeeze a rubber ball very hard.

Â‰ Listen to very loud music.

Â‰ Hold ice in your hand or mouth.

Â‰ Go out in the rain or snow.

Â‰ Take a hot or cold shower.

Â‰ Other:

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Distracting A way to remember these skills is the phrase â€śWise Mind ACCEPTS.â€ť With Activities: 

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Name one body sensation or one fact.
Choose one kind next step or stop for rest.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
